meta.title: Student Health Academy
meta.description: Gamified health literacy for high school: first aid, STIs, vaccines, and more.

hub.title: Student Health Academy
hub.subtitle: Learn in three stages — local, regional, and national. Earn XP, badges, and unlock the next level.
hub.disclaimer: This section is for education only. It is not medical advice and not a substitute for a doctor, nurse, school nurse, or trusted sexual-health clinic. For emergencies, call your local emergency number (e.g. 112 in many European countries).
hub.countryHint: Used only on this device to show relevant context. You can change it anytime.
hub.commandCenterSubtitle: {completed}/{total} lessons complete — keep building real-life health skills.
hub.quizLocked: Complete all modules in this stage to unlock the stage quiz.
hub.quizIntro: Answer at least 70% correctly to unlock the next stage.
hub.allStagesComplete: You completed the full academy path. Keep revisiting lessons to stay sharp.

progress.stageLocked: Locked — complete the previous stage quiz.
progress.allModulesDone: All modules done — take the quiz!

quiz.title: Stage quiz
quiz.needPct: You need at least 70% to unlock the next stage.

lesson.supportText: If this topic connects to something happening in your life, you deserve confidential, respectful support. A trusted adult, school nurse, doctor, sexual-health clinic, or helpline can help you understand your options.

emergency101.title: Calling for help
emergency101.description: The first skill in any emergency is getting professional help fast. Learn what to say and what not to do.
emergency101.tip1: In many European countries you can dial 112 for police, ambulance, or fire — it often works without phone credit.
emergency101.tip2: Stay calm enough to say where you are: address, school name, or visible landmarks. Dispatchers cannot guess your location.
emergency101.tip3: Do not hang up until the operator says you can. They may need more details to send the right help.
emergency101.tip4: If you are not sure it is an emergency, it is still OK to call — describe the situation and follow their advice.
emergency101.fact: Quick access to emergency services saves lives in bleeding, choking, severe allergic reactions, and many other situations.
emergency101.action: With a family member, practice saying your home address and school name out loud as if you were on a call.
emergency101.scenario: A classmate collapses during sports practice. People are shouting, but no one has called for help yet. What are the first three facts you would tell the dispatcher?
emergency101.challenge: Write your home address, school address, and one nearby landmark in your notes app so you can find them fast in an emergency.

bleedingBasics.title: Bleeding: first steps
bleedingBasics.description: Serious bleeding needs pressure and help — not panic. These steps are widely taught in first aid courses.
bleedingBasics.tip1: Wash your hands if you can, or use gloves — protect yourself from blood-borne infections.
bleedingBasics.tip2: Apply firm, steady pressure with a clean cloth or clothing directly on the wound. Do not keep lifting the cloth to peek.
bleedingBasics.tip3: Raise the injured limb above heart level if possible, unless you suspect a broken bone.
bleedingBasics.tip4: If blood soaks through, add another layer on top — do not remove the first cloth.
bleedingBasics.fact: Most life-threatening bleeding can be slowed by strong, continuous pressure until medics arrive.
bleedingBasics.action: Find where the first-aid kit is at school or home. Know who to tell if someone is hurt.
bleedingBasics.scenario: Someone cuts their arm on broken glass after school. The wound is bleeding through a sleeve and they are scared to look at it.
bleedingBasics.challenge: Practice explaining the pressure rule in one sentence: press firmly, add layers, keep pressing until help arrives.

burnsBasics.title: Burns: cool and cover
burnsBasics.description: Small burns need cooling; large or deep burns need emergency care. Avoid harmful home remedies.
burnsBasics.tip1: Cool the burn under cool running water for at least 10 minutes as soon as you can — it reduces pain and depth of injury.
burnsBasics.tip2: Do not use ice directly on the skin; it can cause more tissue damage.
burnsBasics.tip3: Do not put butter, oil, toothpaste, or egg on a burn — they trap heat and increase infection risk.
burnsBasics.tip4: Large burns, burns on face/hands/genitals, or burns from chemicals or electricity need emergency services.
burnsBasics.fact: Proper cooling within minutes can reduce how deep a burn goes — that affects healing and scarring.
burnsBasics.action: Tell one friend: if someone gets burned, cool water for 10 minutes, then cover loosely with clean cloth — no butter.
burnsBasics.scenario: A younger sibling touches a hot pan and starts crying. An adult suggests toothpaste because that is what the family used before.
burnsBasics.challenge: Teach one person the burn rule today: cool running water first, no butter, oil, toothpaste, or egg.

stiBasics.title: STIs: facts, not fear
stiBasics.description: Sexually transmitted infections (STIs) are common and treatable or manageable. Knowing facts reduces stigma and helps people seek care.
stiBasics.tip1: Many STIs have no symptoms at first — testing is the only way to know for sure if you have been exposed.
stiBasics.tip2: Condoms used correctly reduce the risk of many STIs; they do not remove all risk but are an important tool.
stiBasics.tip3: Talking honestly with partners about testing and protection is part of mutual respect — no one should pressure you.
stiBasics.tip4: Youth clinics and doctors offer confidential care in many places; ask what confidentiality means where you live.
stiBasics.fact: Untreated STIs like chlamydia can cause long-term health problems, but testing and treatment are effective when people get care.
stiBasics.action: Save the name of a trusted clinic or helpline for sexual health in your area — even if you do not need it yet.
stiBasics.scenario: A friend heard that you can always tell if someone has an STI by looking at them. They are embarrassed to ask a clinic.
stiBasics.challenge: Find one confidential sexual-health service, clinic, school nurse, or helpline you could recommend without judging them.

consentBasics.title: Consent and boundaries
consentBasics.description: Healthy relationships require clear, ongoing consent. Everyone has the right to say no at any time.
consentBasics.tip1: Consent must be freely given — not pressured by guilt, threats, alcohol, or drugs.
consentBasics.tip2: Silence or not fighting back is not the same as yes. Only an enthusiastic, informed yes counts.
consentBasics.tip3: You can change your mind at any moment. A yes earlier does not mean yes forever.
consentBasics.tip4: If someone crosses your boundaries, you deserve support from a trusted adult, counselor, or helpline.
consentBasics.fact: Schools and youth organizations increasingly teach consent because it prevents harm and builds healthier communities.
consentBasics.action: Think of one person you could talk to if you felt pressured or unsafe — a teacher, relative, or hotline.
consentBasics.scenario: Someone keeps asking for photos after being told no, then says it is proof of love. The pressure is becoming uncomfortable.
consentBasics.challenge: Create a boundary script you could use: “I said no. Stop asking. I am leaving this chat now.” Save it for when emotions run high.

chokingBasics.title: Choking: when to act
chokingBasics.description: A person who cannot breathe needs help immediately. Formal training (e.g. first aid course) is best for back blows and abdominal thrusts.
chokingBasics.tip1: If someone is coughing forcefully, encourage them to keep coughing — do not slap their back randomly.
chokingBasics.tip2: If they cannot speak, breathe, or cough, call emergency services right away.
chokingBasics.tip3: Learn choking relief from a certified instructor — doing it wrong can injure someone.
chokingBasics.tip4: After any choking episode where breathing was blocked, medical evaluation is wise even if they seem fine.
chokingBasics.fact: Bystander action plus EMS response dramatically improves outcomes in complete airway obstruction.
chokingBasics.action: Ask your school if a first aid or CPR course is available and sign up if you can.
chokingBasics.scenario: At lunch, a student suddenly cannot talk and is clutching their throat. Some people think they are joking.
chokingBasics.challenge: Ask a teacher or school nurse where students can learn certified choking first aid or CPR locally.

stiTesting.title: Testing and treatment
stiTesting.description: Getting tested is responsible, not shameful. Treatment works for many bacterial STIs; viral ones can often be managed.
stiTesting.tip1: A sexual health clinic can explain which tests you need — often urine, blood, or a swab.
stiTesting.tip2: Tell clinicians honestly about symptoms or exposures so they test the right things.
stiTesting.tip3: Partners may need testing or treatment too — clinics can advise how to have that conversation safely.
stiTesting.tip4: Finish all antibiotics if prescribed — stopping early can lead to resistant bacteria.
stiTesting.fact: Early treatment prevents complications like pelvic inflammatory disease and reduces spread to partners.
stiTesting.action: Look up one official public health website from your country about STI testing and bookmark it.
stiTesting.scenario: A friend is worried about a possible STI exposure but is afraid the whole community will find out.
stiTesting.challenge: List two privacy questions to ask a clinic: “Who can see my results?” and “Can I be tested confidentially at my age?”

vaccineLiteracy.title: How vaccines work
vaccineLiteracy.description: Vaccines train your immune system using weakened germs, pieces of germs, or instructions (like mRNA) — they do not replace your DNA.
vaccineLiteracy.tip1: Your national schedule lists which vaccines you should get and when — ask your doctor or school nurse.
vaccineLiteracy.tip2: Mild sore arm or fever for a day can happen — it usually means your immune system is responding.
vaccineLiteracy.tip3: Serious allergic reactions are rare and happen soon after the shot — clinics know how to treat them.
vaccineLiteracy.tip4: If you missed a dose, you can often catch up — ask a health professional rather than guessing.
vaccineLiteracy.fact: High vaccination coverage protects whole communities, including people who cannot be vaccinated for medical reasons.
vaccineLiteracy.action: Check whether your HPV or hepatitis B vaccines are up to date with a parent or nurse — and catch up if needed.
vaccineLiteracy.scenario: A viral post claims a vaccine “changes your DNA” and dozens of students are sharing it in a group chat.
vaccineLiteracy.challenge: Compare that claim with your national health ministry or WHO page and write down one trustworthy sentence you could share back.

vpdExamples.title: Vaccine-preventable diseases
vpdExamples.description: Diseases like measles, HPV-related cancers, and hepatitis B are partly preventable through vaccines — understanding them fights myths.
vpdExamples.tip1: Measles spreads very easily and can cause brain inflammation — vaccination has prevented countless deaths worldwide.
vpdExamples.tip2: HPV vaccine is recommended for teens in many countries before sexual activity begins — it reduces cancer risk later.
vpdExamples.tip3: Hepatitis B can become chronic and damage the liver — infant and adolescent doses are part of many schedules.
vpdExamples.tip4: Rumors on social media are not evidence — compare claims with national health ministry or WHO pages.
vpdExamples.fact: Before the measles vaccine, millions of children died each year; vaccines remain one of public health's biggest wins.
vpdExamples.action: Read one short article from your ministry of health about a vaccine on your schedule and note one fact you learned.
vpdExamples.scenario: A school trip is coming up and students are discussing measles, HPV, and hepatitis B, but everyone remembers different facts.
vpdExamples.challenge: Pick one vaccine-preventable disease and make a three-bullet fact card: how it spreads, why it matters, how prevention works.

firstAidReview.title: First aid: putting it together
firstAidReview.description: Review emergencies, bleeding, burns, and when to call for help — readiness beats panic.
firstAidReview.tip1: Start every serious situation with safety for you and others, then call for help if needed.
firstAidReview.tip2: Pressure + elevation (when appropriate) remains the core response for many external bleeds.
firstAidReview.tip3: Burns: cool water first; seek care for large, deep, chemical, electrical, or airway burns.
firstAidReview.tip4: Stay with the person until help arrives if it is safe — reassurance matters.
firstAidReview.fact: Communities where many people know basic first aid respond faster and save more lives before professionals arrive.
firstAidReview.action: Draw a simple flowchart on paper: Emergency? → Call help → Safe to approach? → Basic steps you remember.
firstAidReview.scenario: You are the first calm person at a scene: one student is burned, another is bleeding, and a teacher is calling emergency services.
firstAidReview.challenge: Design a 60-second emergency plan for your classroom: who calls, who gets an adult, who clears space, who stays with the injured person.

stiMyths.title: STI myths vs reality
stiMyths.description: Myths stop people from getting tested. Replace rumors with facts from health authorities.
stiMyths.tip1: Myth: You can always tell who has an STI. Reality: Many infections are invisible without testing.
stiMyths.tip2: Myth: Only certain kinds of people get STIs. Reality: Anyone sexually active can be exposed.
stiMyths.tip3: Myth: Two condoms are safer than one. Reality: Double condoms cause friction and breakage — use one correctly.
stiMyths.tip4: Myth: Antibiotics someone gives you are enough. Reality: Only take medicine prescribed for you after proper diagnosis.
stiMyths.fact: Stigma is a barrier to testing; factual education and confidential care improve outcomes for everyone.
stiMyths.action: If you hear a myth at school, practice responding with: "I think that's a myth — we should check a health site."
stiMyths.scenario: A rumor spreads that only “certain people” get STIs, and a student who might need testing is being mocked.
stiMyths.challenge: Write one stigma-free reply that protects privacy and points people toward testing instead of gossip.

sourceLiteracy.title: Trustworthy health information
sourceLiteracy.description: Learn to spot reliable sources: government health sites, WHO, and recognized hospitals — not random influencers.
sourceLiteracy.tip1: Check who wrote the page, whether it cites studies, and when it was last updated.
sourceLiteracy.tip2: If a claim sounds extreme ("miracle cure", "doctors don't want you to know"), be extra skeptical.
sourceLiteracy.tip3: Use this app's misinformation scanner or similar tools to check viral health claims.
sourceLiteracy.tip4: Your school librarian or biology teacher can often help you evaluate online health pages.
sourceLiteracy.fact: Health literacy — finding and understanding reliable information — is linked to better decisions and outcomes.
sourceLiteracy.action: Compare one TikTok or social claim about health with the same topic on your national health website — note differences.
sourceLiteracy.scenario: A video promises a miracle cure and says doctors hide the truth. It has thousands of likes and dramatic music.
sourceLiteracy.challenge: Use the source check: Who made it? What evidence is shown? What do health authorities say? Decide whether you would share it.

capstoneMix.title: Health literacy capstone
capstoneMix.description: Bring together vaccines, STIs, first aid, and critical thinking about information you see online.
capstoneMix.tip1: Before sharing a health post, ask: source, date, and whether experts agree.
capstoneMix.tip2: Your body: you have the right to ask questions in any medical visit and to bring a trusted person.
capstoneMix.tip3: Prevention stacks: vaccines + safer behaviors + regular checkups when available.
capstoneMix.tip4: Peer support matters — encourage friends to seek real help instead of self-diagnosing online.
capstoneMix.fact: Students who combine knowledge with trusted adults and services navigate health decisions more safely.
capstoneMix.action: Teach one thing you learned in this academy to a sibling or friend in five minutes — teaching deepens memory.
capstoneMix.scenario: You are helping plan a school health week. Students want topics that feel real, not lectures.
capstoneMix.challenge: Create a mini health-week agenda with one first-aid demo, one myth-busting activity, one vaccine literacy task, and one trusted-help resource.

q0.question: What should you tell an emergency dispatcher first if possible?
q0.a1: Where you are and what happened
q0.explanation: Location and a brief description of the emergency help them send the right resources quickly.

q1.question: For heavy bleeding that soaks through a cloth, you should:
q1.a0: Remove the cloth to look at the wound
q1.a1: Add another layer on top and keep pressing
q1.a2: Wait an hour to see if it stops
q1.explanation: Removing the cloth can disturb clots. Add pressure on top until help arrives.

q2.question: For a small fresh burn, the best first step is usually:
q2.a1: Cool with running cool water for several minutes
q2.explanation: Cool running water reduces heat in the tissue. Butter traps heat and increases infection risk.

q3.question: Why might someone get an STI test even without symptoms?
q3.a0: Tests are only for people with symptoms
q3.a1: Many STIs can be silent but still harmful or spreadable
q3.explanation: Many infections have no early symptoms; testing finds them so you can get care and protect partners.

q0.question: If an adult suddenly cannot speak or breathe and holds their throat, you should:
q0.a2: Give them a glass of water
q0.explanation: Complete airway blockage is an emergency — trained help and EMS are needed urgently.

q1.question: If prescribed antibiotics for an STI, you should:
q1.explanation: Stopping early can fail to kill the bacteria and contribute to resistance. Never share antibiotics.

q2.question: Vaccines mainly work by:
q2.a1: Training the immune system to recognize a germ
q2.explanation: Vaccines prepare your immune system so a future encounter with the real germ is much safer.

q3.question: Measles is:
q3.a1: A serious disease that vaccines have greatly reduced
q3.explanation: Measles can cause severe complications; vaccination is a key reason cases dropped worldwide.

q0.question: In a medical emergency at school, the best first step is usually:
q0.a1: Get adult help and emergency services if needed
q0.a2: Wait until the end of class
q0.explanation: Trained adults and EMS are equipped to respond; time matters in emergencies.

q1.question: A reliable place to check vaccine facts is often:
q1.a1: Your country's ministry of health or WHO
q1.explanation: Official health agencies review evidence and update guidance — use them to verify claims.

q2.question: Two condoms at once are:
q2.a1: Not recommended — friction can cause breakage
q2.explanation: Using one condom correctly is safer than double-layering, which increases tear risk.

q3.question: Health literacy means:
q3.a1: Finding, understanding, and using reliable health information
q3.explanation: It is about skills to navigate real information and services, not encyclopedic memorization.
